Okay, so I felt like this was really important to include because it's not always going to be black and white, and there's not always going to be issues where it is appropriate to write someone up, or it might be that you, even upon watching this video, realize that you have someone on your team who is toxic to the rest of them, okay? And so dealing with people is never black and white. We try to make it as much as we can, but I also have to, I want to educate you guys on this so you can make good judgment calls, right? And so as leader, it's your job to protect your team, right? And so sometimes that means that protecting your team from somebody in the team, okay? And so if someone is so toxic on your team that it bothers you, your team probably feels it 10 times to that extent, right? And so a lot of the times I hear comments like this, or I see them in the group, quote, I don't have it in me to deal with Sally today, right? Okay, then my question is, why the heck are you still dealing with Sally? You've been telling me about Sally for three months. Why is Sally still on your team? Tell me a good reason, right? Okay, you need to stop tolerating low performance and bad attitude. Like the world is not full of low performers and people with bad attitude. It is just that you are tolerating the people in your circle to be of that nature, okay? Your life does not have to be surrounded with bad people. You are choosing to allow them in your life, starting with people on your team. So you need to protect and shape your culture by who you hire and fire. You can have all the values. You can have the mission. If you hire and fire the wrong people, it doesn't matter. Like it really won't. If I have the core tenant of Don't Sugarcoat it, and I have five out of seven people working for me that won't tell the truth for the freaking life of them, do you think that we have a culture of Don't Sugarcoat it? No, it sounds like a culture of fake, right? You got to hire people of the same values as you, okay? And that also means firing the people who don't have those values. And so I'm asking you to reflect right now. If you've ever thought to yourself, man, I just wish this person would screw up so I could just have a reason to let them go. Okay. If you have ever thought of that and you have not done anything about it, that is what we call lazy leadership, okay? It's not on them for being a poor performer, for having a bad attitude, for being a toxic teammate. It's on you for being a lazy leader, okay? Because what it is is that it's easier to hold on to someone than it is to make the change because humans don't like change of letting them go, right? It's easier to keep a toxic member on the team than it is to remove and replace them, right? Because oh my gosh, that means I would have to step back into the gym and I would have to pick up extra hours and I would have to do this and this person would do this and like this would, and I can bet you a dollar, I would bet you a lot more than a dollar, that nothing will fall apart. Everything will be fine. You just need to do it. Your mind is being irrational. For some reason, firing is a very emotional thing for people. I promise you once you've done it 50 times, it will not be that way. But in order to get to a place where it doesn't bother you, you've got to rip the evaded off in the first place, okay? So someone's a toxic member on your team. You need to eliminate them immediately. Another phrase you hear often is that's just so and so for you, right? And it's excusing a decision you made a long time ago about what you would tolerate. But because you made that decision say a year ago when you hired them and brought them on, you're not the same person today and you don't have to be. So you don't have to tolerate the same things, okay? And so I urge you to stop tolerating that coming from others on the team, coming from yourself, and I urge you to purge if you need to, okay? Because there's never a right time to completely remake your culture and make the hard firing decisions, and so you might as well do it today. And so the question, the perspective I'd like to put for this is, do you know what it says to others when you do keep those people on your team, okay? It says, if this person can act like shit and act like a terrible employee and act like an asshole and still work here, then why am I trying so hard, right? I can tell you from personal experience, when I was a trainer, I was the top trainer at this gym, and they brought on this girl and I watched her leave early, not clean, not do her programming, all this stuff. And I was like always on top of it. And I remember, and it's funny because it comes back full circle, I remember sitting there thinking one day like, is that really like all they expect of us? Like they roll our herd of work here? Like it made me not want to be there as much because I was a top performer and she was not. It also made me lose respect for the owners because I watched them hold on to her for so long. And so you want their respect from your team, you want to protect your team, you want to provide the A players with the right environment, you need to cut the people who don't support that environment. Okay, so the question is, how do you confront this person if they haven't actually performed poorly? And maybe it's just an attitude issue, right? What if they haven't done anything wrong? Like nothing you could put on paper, nothing tangible, all you could say is like they have such a bad attitude, right? Okay, then you address how they make you feel, okay? It doesn't mean that they did something wrong, it means how you make me feel is uncomfortable, right? And so you don't approach them and say, the team thinks you have a bad attitude, okay? You address what you have observed and then you don't hide behind your team. So you say, I would like to tell you, I think you have a really bad attitude. You don't use your team, you don't put them in front of you, right? You need to prepare for a negative reaction because that's typically the people who need to fire the most will have the worst reaction when you are approaching them about their behavior. But you want to keep the conversations centered around how they make you feel and not what they say in reaction to that because people can hear the words you say but they feel your attitude. And so what you also want to think is like, it will do this person a disservice for the rest of their life if they don't know that their attitude is a problem. And so it's your responsibility to be a truth teller and to tell that person that their attitude is a problem and it will lose them their job. Don't you think that that is what a mentor does, right? So think about Alex and how he coaches you. He tells you the harsh truth, right? That's what you need to do for other people. Whether it means you're letting them go or not, you need to always tell the truth, right? That's how you keep your word. That's how you keep your reputation. And so remember, it's always easier to address this and these issues from early on, okay, rather than let the teammate girl custom to them. So if you see this in the first week or two of bringing someone on, let them go. Like if you're going to build a great team and from the beginning, someone says like within a week, there's two or three things that bother you and you address it and they continue to happen. You just got to cut it.
